# Energy balance

> *How much we take in versus how much we burn — the balance that sets body weight. The goal is
> **recognition**: name the parts of energy expenditure and estimate a person's needs. But hold one
> question as you read — is body weight really just "calories in minus calories out"? Keep it in mind;
> the **Appetite & Weight Regulation** chapter that closes Part I comes back to answer it.*

## Overview

**Energy balance** is the relationship between energy taken in as food and energy
expended. A sustained imbalance drives changes in body weight and metabolic health — though, as the
end of Part I will show, the body does not treat that balance as a passive sum. Total energy
expenditure (TEE) has three components:

$$ \text{TEE} = \text{BMR} + \text{TEF} + \text{PA} $$

- **BMR** — basal metabolic rate, the energy to keep the body running at rest
  (typically **60–70%** of TEE).
- **TEF** — thermic effect of food, the cost of digesting and processing meals (~10%).
- **PA** — physical activity, the **most variable** component between individuals.

::: {.callout-note}
## Clinical pearl
A daily surplus of just ~100 kcal — roughly one sweetened drink — can translate into
clinically meaningful weight gain over a year. Small, *sustainable* changes are the
lever in nutritional counselling.
:::

## Basal metabolic rate

BMR is the largest slice of TEE, driven mainly by **fat-free (lean) mass**. It falls
with age, is lower when thyroid hormone is reduced, and differs by sex (largely via
body composition).

### Estimating BMR: Mifflin–St Jeor

A widely used estimate uses weight ($W$, kg), height ($H$, cm), age ($A$, years) and sex:

$$ \text{BMR}_{\text{women}} = 10W + 6.25H - 5A - 161 $$
$$ \text{BMR}_{\text{men}} = 10W + 6.25H - 5A + 5 $$

Total needs then scale BMR by a **physical-activity level (PAL)** factor — roughly
**1.4** sedentary, **1.6** moderate, **1.8+** very active:

$$ \text{TEE} \approx \text{BMR} \times \text{PAL} $$

::: {.callout-tip}
## Worked example
A sedentary woman, 52 y, 68 kg, 157 cm:
$\text{BMR} = 10(68) + 6.25(157) - 5(52) - 161 \approx \mathbf{1240}$ kcal/day;
total needs $\approx 1240 \times 1.4 \approx \mathbf{1740}$ kcal/day.
:::

## Two previews for later in Part I

Energy balance is not the whole story, and two ideas introduced here are developed later in Part I.

**Which fuel is burning — the respiratory quotient.** You can tell whether the body is running mostly
on carbohydrate or mostly on fat from the gases it exchanges. The **respiratory quotient (RQ)** is the
ratio of carbon dioxide produced to oxygen consumed: it is about **1.0 when burning carbohydrate** and
about **0.7 when burning fat** (a mixed diet sits around 0.85). Here it is enough to know the
definition; *reading* RQ as the body switches between the fed and fasted states is the job of the
**Integrative Metabolism** chapter.

**The body knows how much it has stored.** Body fat is not a passive store. It releases a hormone,
**leptin**, in rough proportion to how much fat you carry — a long-term "fuel gauge" the brain reads to
regulate appetite and defend body weight over months and years. That is the first hint that the tidy
equation above is **defended, not free** — the idea the **Appetite & Weight Regulation** chapter, the
capstone of Part I, returns to in full.

## Check your understanding

A quick self-test on the concepts above before you apply them — your answers are saved
locally in your browser.

<div data-island="quiz" data-src="/quizzes/energy-balance.quiz.yml"></div>

## Practise: estimating a patient's energy needs

Now put it together. Mdm Tan is back to review her diet — work out her energy needs and
turn the estimate into realistic advice. Your choices branch the case.

::: {.callout-tip}
Progress is saved locally — replay to explore the other paths.
:::

<div data-island="case" data-src="/cases/bmr-estimation.case.yml"></div>
